title: High performance carbonized plastics for energy storage device
abstract: Pyrolysis (carbonization) of various plastics, including recycled plastic, can generate carbonaceous materials cheaply and in bulk, which can then be converted into energy storage device materials, e.g., carbon anode active material for Li-ion batteries. The plastic can be dissolved in a suitable solvent or acid, or can be melted. Once liquefied it can be loaded into vessels for extrusion via an electrospinner. Polymer fibers may be formed from the liquefied plastic on the nano- and micro scales, and collectedon a substrate, forming a fabric. These fibers can be converted to high purity carbon and used as electrode materials in batteries and supercapacitors. The fibers can also be coated with Ppy prior to pyrolysis; this helps fibers retain their morphology during carbonization. The fibers can also be loaded with additive particles to enhance their electrochemical performance or alter the composite properties.
claims:
Claims What is claimed is: 1. An energy storage device comprising: a negative electrode comprising carbonized plastic. 2. The energy storage device of claim 1, wherein the carbonized plastic comprises planar graphene crystals. 3. The energy storage device of claim 1, wherein the carbonized plastic comprises planar layers of at least one of graphitic oxide, graphene oxide, or combinations thereof. 4. The energy storage device of claim 3, wherein the carbonized plastic comprises at least one surface including facets indicating the planar layers. 5. The energy storage device of claim 1, wherein the carbonized plastic comprises a plurality of bubble-induced voids. 6. The energy storage device of claim 1, further comprising: a positive electrode, a separator; and an electrolyte, the electrolyte infiltrating the negative electrode, the separator, and the positive electrode. 7. The energy storage device of claim 6, wherein the positive electrode comprises carbonized plastic. 8. The energy storage device of claim 6, wherein the electrolyte comprises at least one of LiPF 6 , LiTFSI, or KOH, or combinations thereof. 9. The energy storage device of claim 6, wherein the positive electrode comprises at least one of metal oxide or metal oxide composites. 10. The energy storage device of claim 1, wherein the plastic comprises a mix of graphitic carbon and non-graphitic carbon and at least one of oxygen or graphene oxide. 11. The energy storage device of claim 10, wherein a ratio of graphitic weight to non graphic carbon weight is selected from a range of 0.01:1 to 0.99:1. 12. The energy storage device of claim 10, wherein a weight percentage of oxygen is selected from a range of 0 wt% to 50 wt%. 13. The energy storage device of claim 10, wherein a weight percentage of graphene oxide is selected from a range of 0% to 95%. 14. The energy storage device of claim 10, wherein a weight percentage of carbon is selected from a range of 80% to 100%. 15. The energy storage device of claim 1, wherein the carbonized plastic comprises at least one of graphitic, graphene oxide, graphite oxide, reduced graphene oxide, reduced graphite oxide layers, or combinations thereof. 16. The energy storage device in claim 1, wherein the carbonized plastic comprises a porosity of 0-10%, and the negative electrode comprises a porosity of 5-50%. 17. The energy storage device in claim 1, wherein the carbonized plastic comprises a particle including at least one of graphitic, graphene oxide, graphite oxide, reduced graphene oxide, reduced graphite oxide layers, or combinations thereof, and the layers defining a feathery rippled texture on a surface of the particle. 18. The energy storage device of claim 1, wherein a crystallinity of the carbonized plastic comprises at least one of amorphous hard carbon type, short-order graphitic (turbostratic) a higher degree of crystallinity selected from 20% to 100% crystalline (graphitized), or combinations thereof. 19. The energy storage device of claim 1, wherein a source of the carbonized plastic comprises post-consumer plastic. 20. The energy storage device of claim 1, wherein the electrode comprises a freestanding electrode consisting essentially of carbonized plastic. 21. The energy storage device of claim 1, wherein the electrode comprises a slurry-casted electrode, and an active material in the slurry-casted electrode comprises the carbonized plastic. 22. The energy storage device of claim 1, wherein the electrode comprises a composite electrode comprising a conductive additive. 23. The energy storage device of claim 1, wherein the negative electrode comprises a composite electrode comprising at least one organic polymer binder. 24. The energy storage device of claim 1, wherein the carbonized plastic comprises a carbon purity selected from a range of 50wt% to l00wt%. 25. A method for fabricating a battery or a supercapacitor, the method comprising the steps of: collecting plastic; rinsing the plastic with a liquid to remove residues; performing a high- temperature carbonization of the plastic; and fabricating a carbonaceous electrode from the carbonized plastic. 26. The method of claim 25, wherein the plastic comprises at least one of consumer, non consumer, recycled, non-recycled plastic, or combinations thereof. 27. The method of claim 25, wherein the plastic comprises at least one of polyethylene terephthalate, polyamides, polycarbonates, polyesters, polyethylenes, polypropylenes, polystyrenes, polyurethanes, polyvinyl chlorides, polyvinylidene chlorides, acrylonitrile butadiene styrenes, polyepoxide trifluorides, polymethyl methacrylates, polytetrafluoroethylenes, phenolics, melamine formaldehydes, urea-formaldehydes, polyetheretherketones, maleimides, polyetherimides, polyimides, plastarches, polylactic acids, furans, silicones, or combinations thereof. 28. The method of claim 25, further comprising performing very low- temperature heat treatment of the plastic. 29. The method of claim 28 wherein the very low-temperature heat treatment of the plastic comprises heating the plastic to a temperature selected from a range of 70Â°C - 250Â°C. 30. The method of claim 25, wherein fabricating the carbonaceous electrode comprises forming a free-standing electrode structure. 31. The method of claim 25, wherein fabricating the carbonaceous electrode comprises adding the carbonized plastic to a slurry and slurry-casting. 32. The method of claim 25, further comprising incorporating the carbonaceous electrode into a battery housing or a supercapacitor housing. 33. The method of claim 25, further comprising performing a low-temperature heat treatment of the plastic. 34. The method of claim 33, wherein the low-temperature heat treatment comprises heating the plastic to a temperature selected from a range of 50Â°C to 450Â°C under inert gas. 35. The method of claim 25, wherein the high-temperature carbonization of the plastic comprises heating the plastic to a temperature selected from a range of 450Â°C to 3500Â°C under inert gas. 36. The method of claim 25, further comprising rinsing the carbonaceous electrode to remove impurities. 37. The method of claim 25, wherein the collected plastic after the rinsing step and prior to low-temperature heat treatment is at least one of dissolved or acidified and subsequently re-precipitated. 38. The method of claim 25, wherein a polymeric property of the collected plastic is different after dissolution or acidification, the polymeric property including at least one of shortened chain length, lengthened chain length, crosslinking, chain cleavage, crystallinity, functionalization, or combinations thereof. 39. An energy storage device comprising: a negative electrode comprising carbon fibers, each fiber having a diameter selected from a range of 10 nm to 500 pm, the carbon fibers defining a material having hierarchically porous carbon, a crystallinity selected from a range of 0 to 99.999%, and a long-range graphitization with graphitic fibers L ranging from 5 pm to 10 mm 

 wherein the carbon fibers are at least one of interwoven, woven, or combination thereof. 40. The energy storage device of claim 39, further comprising: a positive electrode; a separator; and an electrolyte, the electrolyte infiltrating the negative electrode, the separator, and the positive electrode. 41. The energy storage device of claim 40, wherein the positive electrode comprises the carbon fibers. 42. The energy storage device of claim 40, wherein the electrolyte comprises at least one of LiPF 6 , KOH, or combinations thereof. 43. The energy storage device of claim 39, wherein the hierarchically porous carbon comprises micropores having a diameter of <2 nm, mesopores having a diameter selected from a range of 2 to 50 nm, and macropores having a diameter of >50 nm. 44. The energy storage device of claim 43, wherein a volume of the micropores is selected from a range of 1 to 20%, a volume of the mesopores is selected from a range of 1 to 20%, and a volume of the macropores is selected from a range of 2 to 30%. 45. The energy storage device of claim 39, wherein a diameter of the fiber is selected from a range of 10 nm to 500 pm. 46. The energy storage device of claim 39, wherein the negative electrode is a freestanding electrode consisting essentially of the carbon fibers. 47. The energy storage device of claim 39, wherein the negative electrode is a slurry- casted electrode, and the carbon fibers are an active material in the slurry-casted electrode. 48. The energy storage device of claim 39, wherein the negative electrode is a composite electrode comprising a conductive additive. 49. The energy storage device of claim 39, wherein the negative electrode is a composite electrode comprising at least one organic polymer binder. 50. The energy storage device of claim 39, wherein the carbon fibers comprise carbon having a purity of >95% by weight. 51. The energy storage device of claim 39, wherein the carbon fibers further comprise a particulate additive comprising at least one of microparticles, nanoparticles, nanorods, nanofibers, or nanowires comprising at least one of silicon, germanium, a carbon allotrope, sulfur, selenium, nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorus, a metal, a metal oxide, a metal phosphate, a metal sulfide, or combinations thereof. 52. The energy storage device of claim 39, wherein the positive electrode comprises at least one of metal oxide, a metal oxide composite, or combinations thereof. 53. A method for fabricating an energy storage device, the method comprising the steps of: liquefying recycled bottles comprising a plastic by at least one of melting the plastic and dissolving the plastic in a solvent; delivering the liquefied plastic to a conductive tip; applying a high voltage to the conductive tip to extrude plastic fibers therefrom; collecting the extruded plastic fibers on a collection substrate; pyrolyzing the plastic fibers; and forming an electrode for the energy storage device from the pyrolyzed plastic fibers. 54. The method of claim 53, wherein the plastic comprises at least one of polyethylene terephthalate, polyamides, polycarbonates, polyesters, polyethylenes, polypropylenes, polystyrenes, polyurethanes, polyvinyl chlorides, polyvinylidene chlorides, acrylonitrile butadiene styrenes, polyepoxide trifluorides, polymethyl methacrylates, polytetrafluoroethylenes, phenolics, melamine formaldehydes, urea-formaldehydes, polyetheretherketones, maleimides, polyetherimides, polyimides, plastarches, polylactic acids, furans, silicones, or combinations thereof. 55. The method of claim 53, wherein the plastic is liquefied by melting at a temperature of at least 250Â°C. 56. The method of claim 53, wherein the plastic is liquefied by dissolving in the solvent, and the solvent is triflouroacetic acid. 57. The method of claim 53, wherein the conductive tip (i) comprises at least one of a brass alloy, a stainless steel alloy, an aluminum alloy, or a combination thereof, and (ii) defines a fluidic pathway. 58. The method of claim 53, wherein the liquefied plastic is delivered to the conductive tip by a syringe pump. 59. The method of claim 53, wherein the electrode is a carbon anode. 60. The method of claim 53, wherein the electrode is a composite electrode. 61. The method of claim 53, wherein the energy storage device is selected from the group consisting of a battery and a supercapacitor. 62. The method in claim 53, wherein the plastic fibers are pyrolyzed at a temperature selected from a range of 500 to 3500Â°C under inert gas. 63. The method of claim 53, wherein the plastic fibers are pyrolyzed at a high temperature ramp selected from a range of 0.lÂ°C to l000Â°C per second. 64. The method of claim 53, wherein pyrolysis of the plastic fibers comprises inserting the plastic fibers into a quartz tube furnace into a heating zone for rapid pyrolysis. 65. The method in claim 53, further comprising loading particles comprising at least one of microparticles, nanoparticles, nanorods, nanofibers, or nanowires comprising at least one of silicon, germanium, a carbon allotrope, sulfur, selenium, nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorus, a metal, a metal oxide, a metal phosphate, a metal sulfide, or combinations thereof into the liquefied plastic. 66. The method of claim 53, wherein the applied voltage is selected from a range of 5 kV to 40 kV. 67. An energy storage device comprising: a negative electrode comprising a plurality of carbon fibers, wherein the carbon fibers (i) are at least one of interwoven or nonwoven, and (ii) define a material having a crystallinity selected from a range of 0 to 99.999%, a long-range graphitization with graphitic fibers L ranging from 1 pm to 10 mm, and a core-shell structure. 68. The energy storage device of claim 67, further comprising: a positive electrode; a separator; and an electrolyte, the electrolyte infiltrating the negative electrode, the separator, and the positive electrode. 69. The energy storage device of claim 68, wherein the positive electrode comprises the carbon fibers. 70. The energy storage device of claim 67, wherein each fiber comprises hierarchically porous carbon, the porous carbon comprising micropores having a diameter of <2 nm, mesopores having a diameter selected from a range of 2 to 50 nm, and macropores having a diameter of >50 nm. 71. The energy storage device of claim 70, wherein a volume of the micropores is selected from a range of 1 to 20%, a volume of the mesopores is selected from a range of 1 to 20%, and a volume of the macropores is selected from a range of 2 to 30%. 72. The energy storage device of claim 67, wherein each fiber has a diameter selected from a range of 10 nm to 500 pm. 73. The energy storage device of claim 67, wherein the negative electrode is a freestanding electrode consisting essentially of the carbon fibers. 74. The energy storage device of claim 67, wherein the negative electrode is a slurry- casted electrode, and the carbon fibers are an active material in the slurry-casted electrode. 75. The energy storage device of claim 67, wherein the negative electrode is a composite electrode comprising a conductive additive. 76. The energy storage device of claim 67, wherein the negative electrode is a composite electrode comprising at least one organic polymer binder. 77. The energy storage device of claim 67, wherein the carbon fibers comprise carbon having a purity of 95-100% by weight. 78. The energy storage device of claim 67, wherein the carbon fibers further comprise a particulate additive comprising at least one of metal oxides, silicon, silicon oxides, tin, tin oxides, sulfur or combinations thereof. 79. The energy storage device of claim 67, wherein the electrolyte comprises at least one of LiPF 6 , KOH, or combinations thereof. 80. The energy storage device of claim 67, wherein the positive electrode comprises at least one of metal oxide, a metal oxide composite, or combinations thereof. 81. A method for fabricating an energy storage device material, the method comprising the steps of: liquefying a plastic by at least one of melting the plastic or dissolving the plastic in a solvent; delivering the liquefied plastic to a conductive tip; applying a high voltage to the conductive tip to extrude plastic fibers therefrom; collecting the extruded plastic fibers on a collection substrate; impregnating the plastic fibers with FeCl by (i) immersion in FeCl solution, and (ii) drying of the plastic fibers; coating the F eC F - i m p reg n a ted plastic fibers with polypyrrole by exposure to pyrrole vapors; removing at least one of excess FeCF or iron from the polypyrrole-coated plastic fibers; pyrolyzing the polypyrrole-coated plastic fibers; and forming an electrode for the energy storage device from the pyrolyzed plastic fibers. 82. The method of claim 81, wherein the plastic comprises at least one of polyethylene terephthalate, polyamides, polycarbonates, polyesters, polyethylenes, polypropylenes, polystyrenes, polyurethanes, polyvinyl chlorides, polyvinylidene chlorides, acrylonitrile butadiene styrenes, polyepoxide trifluorides, polymethyl methacrylates, polytetrafluoroethylenes, phenolics, melamine formaldehydes, urea-formaldehydes, polyetheretherketones, maleimides, polyetherimides, polyimides, plastarches, polylactic acids, furans, silicones, or combinations thereof. 83. The method of claim 81, wherein the plastic is liquefied by melting at a temperature of at least 250Â°C. 84. The method of claim 81, wherein the plastic is liquefied by dissolving in the solvent, and the solvent is triflouroacetic acid. 85. The method of claim 81, wherein the conductive tip (i) comprises at least one of a brass alloy, a stainless steel alloy, an aluminum alloy, or combinations thereof, and (ii) defines a fluidic pathway. 86. The method of claim 81, wherein the liquefied plastic is delivered to the conductive tip by a syringe pump. 87. The method of claim 81, wherein the electrode is a carbon anode. 88. The method of claim 81, wherein the electrode is a composite electrode. 89. The method of claim 81, wherein the energy storage device is selected from the group consisting of a battery and a supercapacitor. 90. The method in claim 81, wherein the plastic fibers are pyrolyzed at a temperature selected from a range of 500 to 3500Â°C under inert gas. 91. The method of claim 81, wherein the plastic fibers are pyrolyzed at a high temperature ramp selected from a range of 0.9Â°C to 600Â°C per second. 92. The method of claim 81, wherein pyrolysis of the plastic fibers comprises inserting the plastic fibers into a quartz tube furnace into a heating zone for rapid pyrolysis. 93. The method of claim 81, wherein the applied voltage is selected from a range of 5kV to 40kV. 94. The method of claim 81, further comprising loading particles into the liquefied plastic. 95. The method of claim 94, wherein the particles comprise at least one of microparticles, nanoparticles, nanorods, nanofibers, or nanowires comprising at least one of silicon, germanium, a carbon allotrope, sulfur, selenium, nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorus, a metal, a metal oxide, a metal phosphate, a metal sulfide, or combinations thereof. 96. The method of claim 95, wherein the metal comprises at least one of a semimetal, a transition metal, an alkaline earth metal, an alkali metals, or combination or alloy thereof. 97. The method of claim 81 wherein the FeCF solution comprises at least one of water, a polar organic solvent, or combinations thereof. 98. The method of claim 81 wherein the FeCF solution comprises a concentration of FeCF of between 0.01 to 6M FeCF. 99. The method of claim 81 wherein the pyrrole vapors are emitted from a container of liquid pyrrole monomer disposed within a chamber in which the FeCl3-impregnated plastic fibers are disposed. 100. The method of claim 81, wherein the excess FeCF or iron is removed from the polypyrrole-coated plastic fibers by at least one of washing or etching. 101. The method of claim 81 wherein a thickness of the polypyrrole coating is selected from a range of 1 nm to 30 pm. 102. The method of claim 81, wherein pyrolyzing the polypyrrole-coated plastic fibers after excess iron or FeCF are removed produces a core-shell carbon structure. 103. The method of claim 81, wherein the pyrolyzed fibers comprise dispersed particles comprising at least one of microparticles, nanoparticles, nanorods, nanofibers, or nanowires comprising at least one of silicon, germanium, a carbon allotrope, sulfur, selenium, nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorus, a metal, a metal oxide, a metal phosphate, a metal sulfide, or combinations thereof.
